event: The Virgin and Child — engraving after Guercino
symbols: Mary holding the child — the Incarnation, God dwelling in human flesh through the Virgin Mother
infant's lively gesture — the fully human, fully divine Child engaged with the world He came to save
description: A fine line engraving after a painting by Guercino, reproduced by Piranesi, showing the Virgin Mary in a half-figure composition holding the infant Jesus in her arms. Mary wears a veil and flowing robes, looking down at the child with a gentle, slightly sorrowful expression. The nude infant sits on her lap with one arm extended. The engraving is executed with delicate hatching to suggest the soft modeling of Guercino's original, with a light, airy quality. Collector stamps and inscriptions appear at the edges of the print.
theological_description: This engraving reproduces Guercino's tender vision of the Madonna and Child, a subject that encapsulates the central mystery of Christianity: the Incarnation. Mary's slightly sorrowful expression suggests she already intuits the suffering her son will endure, fulfilling Simeon's prophecy in Luke 2:34-35 that a sword would pierce her own soul. The intimate simplicity of the composition — just mother and child — strips the Incarnation to its most essential truth: the infinite God chose to become a helpless infant, dependent on human love. As John 1:14 declares, 'The Word became flesh and made His dwelling among us.' The engraving medium, with its reduction to pure line and tone, perhaps best communicates the theology of divine condescension — God's glory stripped to its simplest, most accessible form. Mary's posture of protective tenderness embodies the vessel chosen to bear the salvation of the world.

scripture_refs.reason: The Word became flesh — the theological truth visible in the divine infant held by His mother
Simeon's prophecy of sorrow — suggested by Mary's pensive, slightly sorrowful expression
God sent His Son born of woman — the Incarnation through Mary

_meta.title: The Virgin and Child in half-figure in her arms, from Guercino
